title: Composting options for flats, balconies, and small urban spaces
subtitle: Comparing bokashi, wormeries, and small cold bins for space-constrained households
excerpt: Flat-dwellers and those with no outdoor space can still divert kitchen waste from landfill. Three systems work without a garden: bokashi fermentation (a sealed bucket for any food waste), a wormery (a stacked box system for vegetable scraps), and a small cold-composting bin suitable for a balcony. This compares their inputs, outputs, and space requirements.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic wormery and small-scale composting guidance. The Worm Research Centre guidance. Bokashi Living UK technical documentation. Zero Waste Network comparison of urban composting methods.

glossaryTerms.definition: An anaerobic fermentation system for kitchen waste using inoculated bran to preserve food scraps rather than decompose them. The pickled material must be buried or added to a compost heap to complete decomposition.
A multi-tray composting system using red brandling worms (Eisenia fetida) to process kitchen vegetable scraps. The worms produce rich castings (worm compost) and a liquid feed.

content.text: UK households send roughly 4.5 million tonnes of food waste to landfill each year. Composting even a portion of kitchen scraps at home diverts that material from a waste stream that generates methane (a greenhouse gas with roughly 80 times the warming effect of CO2 over 20 years in landfill conditions). For flat-dwellers, three systems make this possible without a garden.
Bokashi fermentation
Bokashi

accepts all food waste including meat, fish, and dairy that a wormery or cold bin cannot process. A 10-litre bucket with a sealed lid, sprinkled with inoculated bokashi bran after each addition, ferments the contents over two to four weeks. The result is a pickled, acidic material that must then be buried in soil or added to a large compost heap to complete decomposition. For a flat with no garden, the local community garden, allotment, or urban farm will often accept bokashi output.
Space required: one 10-litre bucket under the kitchen sink. Two buckets allow continuous use (fill one while the other matures).
Running cost: bokashi bran costs roughly 5-8 pounds per kilo. One kilo lasts a single-person household several months.
Accepts: all kitchen waste including cooked food, meat, fish, and dairy.
Output: fermented food mass that needs burial or a compost heap to finish. Also produces a leachate liquid usable as a drain cleaner or diluted plant feed.
Wormery
Wormeries

work year-round at indoor temperatures (15-25°C) and produce rich worm castings usable directly as potting compost or soil amendment. The worms process vegetable scraps, fruit peel, coffee grounds, and small amounts of cardboard. They cannot process meat, citrus, onion, or dairy.
Space required: a stacked tray system fits in a 400 x 400 mm footprint; suitable for a kitchen corner or balcony (above 5°C). Starter kits include a suitable container and worms.
Running cost: minimal after purchase; worms self-replicate.
Accepts: vegetable scraps, fruit peel (not citrus in quantity), coffee grounds, loose tea, torn cardboard, eggshells.
Output: worm castings (potting compost), worm leachate (liquid feed, diluted 10:1).
Small cold-composting bin (balcony)

A 50-litre enclosed plastic bin on a south-facing balcony can manage vegetable scraps from a one- or two-person household. Decomposition is slower than a garden bin (months rather than weeks) but the material eventually composts. The bin must be sheltered from frost; below -5°C, decomposition halts entirely. In a mild UK city, balcony composting is viable year-round.
Space required: 50-litre bin, 500 mm x 400 mm x 700 mm.
Accepts: vegetable and fruit scraps, coffee grounds, dry leaves and paper.
Output: cold compost suitable for container potting mixes. Annual output for a one-person household: 5-10 litres of finished material.
Choosing between the three
Meat, fish, dairy in the waste stream: bokashi only.
Want finished compost for houseplants or containers: wormery.
Have a balcony and primarily vegetable waste: cold bin.

Full waste stream, no garden, no place to drop off bokashi output: use bokashi first, then a wormery as a second stage. Mix a little garden lime into the acidic bokashi to reduce the acidity, then add it to a mature wormery.

attrs.cause: Normal; bokashi fermentation is anaerobic and produces lactic acid. A vinegary smell is correct. A putrid or rotten smell indicates the bran was insufficient or the bucket was not fully sealed.
attrs.fix: Add more bran, press the contents down to expel air pockets, seal the lid firmly. If already putrid, the batch has failed and should be buried or disposed of.

attrs.symptom: Wormery is producing liquid faster than expected
attrs.cause: High-moisture kitchen scraps (melon, cucumber, tomatoes) are releasing more free liquid than the bedding can absorb
attrs.fix: Add shredded damp cardboard or newspaper between layers to absorb excess moisture. Drain the leachate more frequently.
